gallery.text.1: Supports made from CCA-treated pine. Framing timber was used to build joists. Rafters and battens were reclaimed from an old shed that had, in turn, been built from reclaimed materials but was demolished to make way for another structure. The bricks (actually pavers) from the old shed were used for the floor.
gallery.text.2: Floor and sunken walkway made from pavers that had been recovered from an old shed.
gallery.text.3: Rear wall, made from reclaimed plywood packaging, prior to cavities being built and filled with clay-soil.
gallery.text.4: A view across the back and Eastern side during construction. The black `pole' is the flue.
gallery.text.5: A view from inside, across the North Western corner during construction.
gallery.text.6: Near completed greenhouse. Supports for the seedling box shelf can be seen to the upper right. Recovered glass was used for the lower front wall, and fixed in 35x90 pine. Planter boxes are made from recovered materials where possible. My furnace is making a cameo appearance as it avoids inclement weather.
gallery.text.7: Reclaimed glass being cut to specification for window above the annexe.
gallery.text.8: Old school linseed and lime putty was used to fix the window in the frame above the annexe.
gallery.text.9: Side view of the annexe that houses the thermal mass heater whilst still under construction.
gallery.text.10: Completed annexe. The extended roof serves to keep rain from entering the feed pipe for the heater.
